coredata.dc:title: Efficient eradication of antibiotic and dye by C-dots@zeolite nanocomposites: Performance evaluation, and degraded products analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Rathi, Aanchal
Basu, Soumen
Barman, Sanghamitra
coredata.dc:description: Metronidazole (MET), a recalcitrant antibiotic from the nitro-imidazole family and commercially used Rhodamine B (RhB) dye, contributes a huge to water pollution, which needs to eliminate, preferably by photocatalytic degradation technique. The Cdots@zeolite (CDZ) nanocomposites with different weight ratios (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, 1:7) were synthesized hydrothermally to degrade MET and RhB molecules. The CDZ composites were characterized by XRD, BET, EDS, and XPS technique which verifies the crystalline nature, incorporation of C-dots into zeolite frameworks with high surface area (∼187 m2/g). The morphology, d-spacing and lattice planes were analyzed by SEM images, HR-TEM and SAED analysis. The maximum degradation (∼79%) was achieved at an optimum catalyst dose of 0.2 g/L and pH 4 for MET and that of RhB was ∼90% at a catalyst dose of 0.4 g/L. The PZC (point of zero charge) value for CDZ composite was about pH 3.4, which justifies the maximum removal of MET at pH 4. The obtained rate constants ‘k’ were found to be 0.0081, 0.0041, and 0.0101 min−1 in sun, UV, and visible light sources, respectively. The real industrial wastewater sample has been treated to give ∼68% of COD and ∼62% TOC removal. Moreover, the intermediates of plausible degradation pathways were identified by the m/z values obtained from GC-MS analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metronidazole (MET), a recalcitrant antibiotic from the nitro-imidazole family and commercially used Rhodamine B (RhB) dye, contributes a huge to water pollution, which needs to eliminate, preferably by photocatalytic degradation technique. The Cdots@zeolite (CDZ) nanocomposites with different weight ratios (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, 1:7) were synthesized hydrothermally to degrade MET and RhB molecules. The CDZ composites were characterized by XRD, BET, EDS, and XPS technique which verifies the crystalline nature, incorporation of C-dots into zeolite frameworks with high surface area (∼187 m2/g). The morphology, d-spacing and lattice planes were analyzed by SEM images, HR-TEM and SAED analysis. The maximum degradation (∼79%) was achieved at an optimum catalyst dose of 0.2 g/L and pH 4 for MET and that of RhB was ∼90% at a catalyst dose of 0.4 g/L. The PZC (point of zero charge) value for CDZ composite was about pH 3.4, which justifies the maximum removal of MET at pH 4. The obtained rate constants ‘k’ were found to be 0.0081, 0.0041, and 0.0101 min−1 in sun, UV, and visible light sources, respectively. The real industrial wastewater sample has been treated to give ∼68% of COD and ∼62% TOC removal. Moreover, the intermediates of plausible degradation pathways were identified by the m/z values obtained from GC-MS analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Metronidazole (MET) antibiotic comes under the nitro-imidazole category, having a strong affinity towards the contagious effect of bacteria (anaerobic and protozoan) like Giardia lamblia and Trichomoniasis vaginalis (Nasseh et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). It can be transferred to sewage waste by urine and stools and its metabolites have carcinogenic, mutagenic and genetic toxicity effects (Huang et al., 2011). A report has been made which suggests that antibiotic manufacturing constitutes about 15% of the total manufactured drugs in the whole world (Nasseh et al., 2020). In the modern era, these medications of antibiotics have got a lot of attention due to the high risk of infectious diseases in human beings and animals. It is widely consumed for the treatment of anaerobic protozoan infections and found in wastewater runoff from the laboratory, hospital, and pharmaceutical waste (Ao et al., 2021). These antibiotics are available in the environment in the range of mg/L to ng/L and wastewater treatment systems have not efficiently eliminated these contaminants (Nasseh et al., 2020; Pradyasti et al., 2018). The rhodamine-B (RhB) dye is a fluorescent xanthene compound which is highly used in dyeing industries and is highly soluble in water. It causes many harmful effects like nausea, nasal/eye/skin problems, itching, neuro or gastrointestinal disorders and reproductive toxicity (Sharma and Basu, 2021a). There is a keen need to control or eliminate effectual MET and RhB content and its traces from the foods or drinks which turned into a hot area of research (Chen et al., 2018; N and Kumar, 2017; Yuan et al., 2016).

Many techniques have been implemented to eradicate noxious pollutants from the environment such as flocculation, adsorption, advanced oxidation process (AOPs) (Duan et al., 2020; Singh et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2020), coagulation, membrane separation, and anaerobic/aerobic degradation (Rathi et al., 2019; Sharma and Basu, 2021b). Though, these mentioned techniques have inadequate elimination potential, are highly expensive, formation of secondary metabolites/pollutants, and are tedious also. While the photocatalysis technique employing semiconductor materials has attracted the intellect of researchers as a green method (Li et al., 2018). These photocatalysts can give complete mineralization of the noxious pollutants and also have a tendency to function under natural sunlight (light source) which costs extremely low (Pirsaheb et al., 2018; Pourata et al., 2009). The appropriate use of a wide solar spectrum, effective surface reduction-oxidation reactions, and rapid charge carrier movements were the core demand for the photocatalytic process to occur. To accomplish these properties, the eco-friendly and harmless C-dots (carbon dots) fit very well in the category of the promising photocatalyst.

The C-dots are nanostructured carbon material (size ranges between 1 and 10 nm), which possess strong and tunable chemical properties. They are photoluminescent nanoparticles possessing very little toxicity, potent and compatible structures with high stability, and interesting photocatalytic traits (Liu et al., 2017; Mu et al., 2016). These interesting properties provide a wide range of applications like sensing (Sohal et al., 2020), drug delivery, bioimaging, photocatalysis, and electrocatalysis. The C-dot structures have delocalized conjugated π-bonds atmosphere which promotes fast charge separation and photo-induced charge carrier movement, finally improves the photocatalytic efficiency (Pirsaheb et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2011). The carbon of C-dots is sp2/sp3 hybridized which holds the fine electron transferring or storage property (Di et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2016). They can act as both efficient photocatalyst and photovoltaic cells because they have a fine light absorber property (Li et al., 2017). A new temperature dependant/controlled fluorescent particle has been synthesized by introducing C-dots into the templates of polyvinyl alcohol, potassium alum, and polyurethane (Dong et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2016; Tan et al., 2016). These templates can hinder the vibrations and rotations of the exposed functional/active groups on C-dots at the intramolecular level, and hence stabilizes the triplet excited states. On the other hand, these C-dots are small crystal-like structure and gives lesser yield on synthesis in batches, while their reusability is not an easy task and time taking also. These C-dots are rich in surface active groups which promote its attachment to the other materials for forming potent composite materials. These C-dots have some demerits like separation problem, decrease in efficiency due to aggregation/oxidation (Samadi-Maybodi and Sadeghi-Maleki, 2018); therefore C-dot composites are promoted.

Zeolites are porous aluminosilicate structures with high chemical and thermal stability, and an appropriate nano-material for incorporating C-dots into the zeolite frameworks “dots in zeolites” (Zhang et al., 2020). The fabrication of guest photoluminescent nanomaterial into well-defined pores/cavities of zeolite required a strong driving force to accumulate guest and host (Zhang et al., 2020). Favorably the solvothermal and hydrothermal synthesis methods seemed to be appropriate, for the preparation of the series of blue emissive C-dots@zeolite (CDZ) composites. This research article focuses on the in-situ preparation of different ratios of CDZ composites for eliminating pollutants by photocatalytic degradation phenomena such as MET antibiotic and a model dye, Rhodamine B. The recent progress in photocatalyst composites using C-dots was studied and summarized. The effect of various parameters such as the effect of dose, pH, scavenger, and time study was explored along with the COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand), TOC (Total Organic Carbon), and intermediate product analysis. These higher medications cause a serious threat to the environment and human wellbeing. So, there is a need to tackle this situation by treating the antibiotic waste with harmless photocatalysts.

The precursor catechol was dissolved in a specific amount of methanol (MeOH). After complete dissolution, the solution becomes transparent. Then the transparent solution was shifted to the Teflon-lined autoclave of appropriate volume to get treated hydrothermally and tightened in the stainless-steel container. Then the autoclave was heated at 180 °C temperature for 12 h. The reaction was cooled at the room temp, washed with PET ether, centrifuged, and dried to obtain C-dots (Yang et al., 2021).

The series of five different CDZ composites were prepared by varying the weight ratios of C-dots: zeolite (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, 1:7). The specific amount of precursor catechol and 200 mg of the zeolite were mixed with the solvent and stirred for 6–7 h for homogenous distribution. Then the reaction mixture was kept in stainless steel hydrothermal autoclave at 180 °C temperature for 12 h. Then it was cooled to room temp, washed with PET ether, centrifuged, and dried to obtain powdered CDZ composite (Yang et al., 2021). The general synthesis scheme for the CDZ composite was shown in Scheme S1.

Different ratios of CDZ (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, 1:7) were inspected for their catalytic efficiency against the noxious contaminants (MET and RhB). The dose of CDZ photocatalyst was varied from 0.05 to 0.25 g/L, and poured into a specific volume of the contaminant solution of MET (10 ppm), and stirred for 40 min in dark conditions to accomplish the state of equilibrium for further degradation activity. The MET solution was supplied with various light sources (UV/sunlight/visible) and the treated solution was checked at different time intervals to know the kinetics and rate constants. To examine the catalytic activity, the post-treatment solution of MET was withdrawn, filtered and the value was checked at its λ max 320 nm (Nasseh et al., 2020). The HACH DR 5000 UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used for recording the absorbance (ABS) data to know the change in MET concentration. The optimum experimental parameters were aimed to inspect by varying the photocatalyst dose, pH of the solution, time, light sources, and kinetics. The intensities of visible (45 W CFL lamp), UV (100 W Hg lamp), and sunlight sources were ∼100 W/m2, 46–48 W/m2, and 600 W/m2 respectively. To know the responsible species for pollutant degradation the scavenging experiment was also carried out. The experimental studies were performed in triplicates for accuracy.

The formula (Eq. (1)) used for calculating the degradation efficiencies are ascribed below:
(1)
Degradation
( % )

ABS i

Where ABSi is the initial absorbance and ABSt is the absorbance of MET contaminant at a specified time ‘t’. Moreover, the role and formation of responsible radicals were analyzed by using different chemicals (ascorbic acid, methanol, DMSO) as scavengers. About 10−3 M scavenger solutions were prepared before degradation treatment and catalyst addition.

The XRD pattern of C-dots, zeolite, and CDZ are depicted in Fig. 1 a. The characteristic peaks were shown at 2Ө value of 6.55°, 8.70°, 9.76°, 13.51°, 15.29°, 19.67°, 22.42°, 25.75°, 26.42°, 27.68°, 31.00° for MOR which corresponded to (110), (020), (200), (111), (310), (330), (150), (202), (350), (132), (402) lattice planes of zeolite (JCPDS no. 430171), respectively. These spectra of composites revealed that they retained the zeolite framework structures even after the incorporation of C-dots. The XRD spectrum of C-dots (inset of Fig. 1a) has a broad peak at 24.04° which corresponds to the (002) plane. The XRD peak of C-dots was merged with the peaks of zeolite in the case of CDZ nanocomposites. The broadness in the XRD spectrum confirmed the small size of the C-dots while sharp peaks indicated that the crystallinity of the zeolite was not disturbed.

The surface area analysis of as-prepared catalysts was done by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm. The specific surface area of 1:5 CDZ is 187 m2/g, zeolite (500 m2/g), and other composites are tabulated in Table S1. The CDZ composites and zeolite possess type-IV isotherm (Fig. 1b). The surface area was decreased due to the incorporation of non-porous C-dots into the porous zeolite framework. From the BJH plots (Fig. 1c), it has been seen that after fabrication with C-dots the intensity of macro-pores increased in the ratio of 1:5 CDZ composites while in the case of zeolite the macro-pores were comparatively less. The micro-pores are also available in the zeolite and prepared CDZ composites (Rathi et al., 2021).

The XPS characterization technique revealed the chemical states of the present elements and their availability (oxygen, carbon, silicon, aluminum elements) in the as-prepared photocatalyst. The survey spectrum of the 1:5 CDZ composite possesses peaks of all the mentioned elements (O, C, Si, and Al) as shown in Fig. 1d. The structural properties (bonds b/w the elements) of the CDZ composite were also predicted by XPS analysis, the peak of carbon was deconvoluted into 2 major peaks i.e., 284.63 eV and 286.05 eV which correspond to C–C/CC and C–O (Fig. 1e), respectively (Liu et al., 2017, 2019), while the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) has the deconvoluted peaks at 530.8 eV and 532.68 eV confirming the presence of CO/O–Al and Si–O/C–O–C in CDZ nanocomposite (Fang et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2019; Nocuń and Kwaśny, 2012).

The morphological analysis was carried out with SEM and HRTEM characterizations. Based on the SEM-EDS data, it can be seen that the C-dots have been successfully fabricated into the zeolite framework. Fig. 1g depicted the SEM morphology of 1:5 CDZ composite having aggregation of small particles with a rough surface. Fig. 1h shows the elemental composition of the 1:5 CDZ composite, and the availability of carbon elements indicates the presence of C-dots in the zeolite framework and distribution of C-dots over the zeolite. The % elemental composition from EDS analysis also gave an idea about the elements like carbon from the C-dots and total oxygen from (hydroxyl group of C-dots + alumino-silicates zeolite frameworks).

From HRTEM images, d-spacing values of C-dots, zeolite, and 1:5 CDZ composite have been calculated using ImageJ software (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2a indicated that the incorporation of C-dots has been successfully taken place inside the zeolite channels. The d-spacing value of C-dots was found to be 0.24 nm indicating the (224) plane as shown in Fig. 2a and b. The CDZ composites have planes of both C-dots and zeolite as shown in Fig. 2. For zeolite, 1.35, 1.02, 0.90, 0.65 nm d-spacing value with (110), (200), (310) and (330) planes were depicted in Fig. 2c–e (Rathi et al., 2021).

For a composite to be an efficient photocatalyst, it must possess greater light-absorbing properties with a narrow bandgap, which results in decreased recombination of photoinduced e−/h+ pairs. The UV-DRS method was used to identify the absorption wavelength range of the 1:5 CDZ composite and C-dots. The highly intense absorbance bands of C-dots and CDZ composites (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, 1:7) were lied in the 240–350 nm range, indicating them as an UV-active materials (Fig. S1a). In addition, Tauc's equation (Eq. (2)) was applied to calculate the bandgap energy of the synthesized materials (Kundu et al., 2021; Monga and Basu, 2021; Sharma and Basu, 2021c).
(2)
( α h υ )
1 / 2
= h υ
−
E g

The absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, and bandgap energy were designed by α, h, ν, and Eg, respectively. The bandgap energy of C-dots, 1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, 1:7 CDZ composites were found to be 2.59 eV, 2.26 eV, 2.40 eV, 2.57 eV, 2.49 eV, 2.43 eV, respectively as depicted in Figs. S2a–f. The narrow bandgap of 1:5 CDZ composite implies the greater light-absorbing character due to the decline in recombination of electron/hole pairs which results in better photocatalytic efficiency than other composites.

The photoluminescence (PL) study suggested that the photocatalytic activity of the prepared composite is greatly dependant on the recombination rate of e−/h+ charged species. The PL data of the C-dots and CDZ (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, 1:7) nanocomposite were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm as shown in Fig. S1b. The lower intensity in the PL spectrum indicated the low recombination rate of electrons/holes which leads to better charge separation. The peak intensity of C-dots and other composites (1:1, 1:3, 5:1, 1:7) was higher as compared to 1:5 CDZ composite, indicating the lower recombination rate with better charge separation of electron/hole pairs and thereby gives better degradation efficiency than others. The reason for the better charge separation in the composite may be due to the formation of heterojunctions which lead to improved e− transfer. At the surface of the zeolite in CDZ composite, the transfer of photo-induced electrons is improved due to the electrical conductance between the zeolite and C-dot surface. Therefore, the as-prepared 1:5 CDZ composite has suitable optical properties which made it an efficient photocatalyst that resulted in better photocatalytic activity (Monga and Basu, 2021; Sharma and Basu, 2021c; You-ji and Wei, 2011).

The kinetic analysis of the as-prepared ratios of CDZ composites was investigated and their rate constants were calculated by Eq. (3). The parameter ‘k’ indicates the rate constant, C0 and C are the concentrations of the noxious contaminants at a time, t = 0 and t respectively (Monga and Basu, 2019; Sharma and Basu, 2020).
(3)
ln
( C/C0 )
=
− kt

Fig. 3 a depicts the catalytic activities of all the composites (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, and 1:7) along with bulk zeolite powder and C-dots for 150 min under light irradiations. The rate constants on different catalysts were determined from the intercept of the graph, ln(C/C0) versus time, and shown in Fig. 3b. The ‘k’ values for zeolite, C-dots, CDZ (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, and 1:7) are 8.5 × 10−6, 3.31 × 10−4, 0.0033, 0.0042, 0.0051, 0.00243 and 0.00524 min−1, respectively. The 1:5 CDZ composite was found to have the maximum rate constant and % degradation (64.85%) due to its high surface area, pore-volume, low recombination rate, and suitable bandgap energy value.

The 1:5 CDZ composite was further investigated for the effect of catalyst dose from 0.05 g/L to 0.25 g/L (Fig. 3c). The increased amount of composite lead to an increase in the photocatalytic efficiency i.e. from 0.05 g/L to 0.2 g/L and then a slight decrease was observed. This decline of degradation efficiency might be due to the saturation and opacity of the contaminant solution resulting in the blockage of active sites (Kundu et al., 2021; Monga et al., 2020).

The optimum pH for MET degradation (Fig. 3d) and point of zero charge (PZC) was also determined for the 1:5 CDZ composite. The PZC value was evaluated by the salt addition method reported by Bakatula and coworkers (Bakatula et al., 2018). From the graph, ΔpH (pHf – pHi) versus pHi, the PZC value of the optimum photocatalyst was found to be ∼ pH 3.4 (Fig. 3e). The effect of pH was investigated from pH 1–11 and the % degradation was found to be the best in the acidic environment i.e. nearby pH 4. This is because the catalyst's surface becomes positively charged when pH is greater than PZC and attracts the negatively charged MET contaminant (Nasseh et al., 2020; Rathi et al., 2021). The efficiency of catalyst decreases in the alkaline medium due to excess of OH− species available on the catalyst surface making it negatively charged due to which deactivation of active sites occurs (Sharma and Basu, 2020).

For comparison, different light sources (UV, sunlight, and visible) were used to find the degradation efficiency of the specific pollutants. Fig. 3f illustrates the kinetic analysis and rate constant of MET under various light sources. The photocatalysis followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics and their ‘k’ values were found in the order, visible > sunlight > UV and the values are tabulated in Table S2. The significance of the illuminating area was also explored by taking equal amounts of MET pollutant in 4 containers of different diameters. The exposed surface areas of the pollutant were calculated to be 2.5, 5, 10, 15 cm2 (constant distance was maintained for the pollutant's upper layer to visible light source in each container). The % degradation was observed to increase with an increase in surface area (Fig. 4 a) (Aanchal et al., 2020).

The real industrial wastewater degradation was tested with 1:5 CDZ composite for its photo-activity. The COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand) and TOC (Total Organic Carbon) analyses were done to quantify the organic pollutants of the wastewater. The removal percentage of COD and TOC were calculated by using the formulas: % COD = [(CODi - CODf)/CODf] × 100%; and %TOC = [(TOCi - TOCf)/TOCf] × 100%, respectively; where CODi and TOCi are the initial COD and TOC, while CODf and TOCf are the final COD and TOC of raw wastewater. The % TOC and % COD removal data were recorded at 60, 120, 180, and 240 min time intervals (Fig. 4b.) by treating the 50 ml of raw wastewater with 0.4 g/L of the catalyst. The total time of degradation was increased for TOC and COD analysis to check the photo-activity of CDZ composite towards the real wastewater. The maximum % COD removal of ∼67.8% and % TOC removal of 62.2% was achieved, indicating enhanced mineralization of organic matter present in wastewater.

RhB industrial dye (5 ppm) was also degraded with 1:5 CDZ nanocomposite for comparison of the photocatalytic efficiency, and its absorbance was recorded at λ max 554 nm (Monga and Basu, 2019). The effect of catalyst dose (0.1–0.5 g/L) of 1:5 CDZ composite was investigated (Fig. 4c) by stirring it for 40 min in dark and then 120 min under light source. The catalytic dose of 0.4 g/L gave maximum of ∼90% degradation, indicating 1:5 CDZ composite was also applicable for RhB dye. The experimental studies were performed in triplicates for accuracy. The comparison for the photocatalytic degradation of MET and RhB by various photocatalyst materials was made in Table 1 .

The stability of the CDZ composite has significance for its practical applicability. The regenerability of the composite was investigated for few cycles by simple washing with distilled water and dried at 60 °C temperature. Fig. S3a indicates the data of reusability of 1:5CDZ photocatalyst up to 4 cycles, and a slight decrease in the % degradation (up to 10–15%) was observed. This study reports the higher stability of CDZ materials which is a promising photoactive material with its relevancy for the pollutants elimination from the environment. To ensure the original properties of the CDZ composite, characterizations like XRD, BET, BJH, and SEM after the photocatalysis treatment were done. The after-treatment XRD spectrum of 1:5 CDZ composite possesses the actual peaks with a slight decrease in the intensity, indicating that the crystallinity and planes of the composite were not disturbed even after the photocatalytic reaction and depicted in Fig. S3b. The after-treatment surface area was measured by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm which shows the minor decrease in the surface area from 187 to 163 m2/g and pore volume from 0.244 to 0.229 cm3/g. The after-treatment BET analysis (Fig. S3c) confirmed that the type-IV isotherm was also maintained. While the presence of micro and macropores in 1:5 CDZ composite after the treatment were analyzed from BJH analysis (Fig. S3d). The surface morphology was also verified from SEM analysis, indicating that the particles remain intact even after the photocatalysis reaction as depicted in Figs. S3e and f. This substantiates that, the CDZ composite is highly regenerable and can be applied to large-scale reactions.

The role of scavengers has been studied to know the active species responsible for the degradation of MET contaminant and the degradation mechanism was also explored. In scavengers study, the various active compounds, like ascorbic acid (AA), methanol (MeOH), and DMSO have been used to trap superoxide radicals, holes, and hydroxyl radicals (Monga et al., 2020; Rosenblum and El-Sabban, 1982; Shao et al., 2018, 2020), respectively. Fig. 4d indicates that holes and •OH are the responsible species for the degradation of MET as the % degradation of MeOH and DMSO obtained are much lesser than that of other scavenger and even no-scavenger.

The surface of the CDZ composite absorbs light, greater than its band-gap energy value which makes an excitation from VB (valence band) to CB (conduction band). The photo-generated electrons got excited to VB leaving holes (h+) behind. The generated h+ reacted with OH− to form •OH radical species (Nagar and Basu, 2021). The reported approximate potentials of CB and VB for C-dots are −0.09eV and 2.5 eV, respectively (Yang et al., 2021). The VB potential of C-dots are more positive and greater than the oxidation potential of H2O/•OH (i.e. 2.38 eV), confirming that the holes (h+) can oxidize the water molecules to •OH radicals and are the major responsible species for photodegradation of MET. This radical-mediated oxidation plays a great role in the photocatalytic degradation of noxious organic contaminants (Shao et al., 2018, 2020). Whereas, the electron reduction of oxygen in this case is less/not feasible due to its less CB potential than that of O2/O2 •- (i.e. −0.33 eV) (Sharma and Basu, 2021c). The schematic representation of the photodegradation mechanism is displaced in Scheme 1 .

The degraded intermediate products of MET were analyzed with GC-MS spectrum by m/z ratios. The organic intermediates were extracted by organic solvent methanol, filtered, and then used for the GC-MS analysis. The GC-MS spectrum in Fig. S4 was analyzed and the plausible pathway for MET degradation was proposed (Fig. S5) by attacking e−/h+ pairs or hydroxyl species in the photodegradation phenomenon (Dai et al., 2016; Martins et al., 2021; Xia et al., 2019). The drawn plausible pathway was similar to that of the previous work, suggesting the hydroxyethyl cleavage takes place along with nitro-reduction, de-methylation, and de-nitration steps (Dai et al., 2016; El Bouraie and Ibrahim, 2021; Xu et al., 2019). The MET molecules get converted into 2-methyl-5-nitro-1H-imidazole (m/z = 126.03) by hydroxyethyl cleavage which upon de-nitration forms 2-methyl-1H-imidazole (m/z = 82.05). The 2-methyl-5-nitro-1H-imidazole leads to the formation of 1H-imidazole-5-amine (m/z = 85.05) through nitro-reduction and attacked by •OH radicals to form 2-methyl-1H-imidazole-4,5-dione (m/z = 112.03). The 2-methyl-1H-imidazole-4,5-dione undergoes fragmentation to form N-ethylidene formamide (m/z = 71.04) and is also attacked by •OH radicals to give rise to simpler hydrocarbons after several steps (Sharma and Basu, 2021c). The 2-methyl-5-nitro-1H-imidazole also undergoes de-methylation and de-nitration to produce 1H-imidazole (m/z = 68.04) and then after several hydroxyl attacks 2-aminoacetonitrile (m/z = 56.02), 2-hydroxyacetonitrile (m/z = 57.02), acetic acid were formed. On the other hand, the MET molecules directly undergo de-nitration to form 2-(2-methyl-1H-imidazole-1-yl)ethan-1-ol (m/z = 126.08) which upon de-methylation forms 2-(1H-imidazole-1-yl)ethan-1-ol (m/z = 112.06). After the ring-opening of 2-(1H-imidazole-1-yl)ethan-1-ol, few intermediates like, 1-ethyl-1H-imidazole (m/z = 96.07), acetamide (m/z = 59.04) were formed and mineralized to simpler hydrocarbons. The several intermediate products can undergo an oxidation process by hydroxyl attack, give rise to carboxylic acids that are ultimately mineralized into simpler hydrocarbons, CO2, NO2, NH3, and water (Ammar et al., 2016; Asgharzadeh et al., 2020; Chiarinelli et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2019). These formed intermediates were in agreement with the reported literature. The intermediates formed at a retention time (RT) of 29.98 were analyzed by m/z values (Fig. S6) and drawn in the form of a scheme shown in Fig. S5.

The C-dots were successfully fabricated on the zeolite framework via a hydrothermal method and were exploited for recalcitrant MET and RhB degradation. The facile method of fabricated CDZ composite provides the formation of 5 CDZ composites of different ratios (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 5:1, 1:7) with fascinating photo-activity and sufficient surface area for adsorption and photodegradation of noxious contaminants. The 1:5 CDZ composite gave maximum photodegradation efficiency due to its high surface area, pore-volume, low recombination rate, and suitable bandgap energy value. The pH results were well justified with the PZC point which was determined by the salt addition method. The rate constants or % degradation data of MET under different light sources conclude that the maximum elimination was achieved in visible light. The scavenger study reveals that the hydroxyl radicals were majorly responsible for the photodegradation of pollutants. The GC-MS analysis was carried out for the identification of the intermediates and the scheme for a plausible degradation pathway was drawn. This work gives a new route to synthesize an efficient zeolite-based photoactive composite for MET elimination from wastewater effluent and can be very beneficial to eradicate the pharmaceuticals from water bodies.

Aanchal Rathi designed and performed the experiments, analyzed data and co-wrote the paper. Soumen Basu: supervised the research, designed experiments and co-wrote the paper. Sanghamitra Barman: supervised the research, designed experiments and co-wrote the paper.
